title: Regulatory grammar in human promoters uncovered by MPRA-based deep learning
methods: convolutional neural network (CNN) with self-attention pooling
genetic algorithm for synthetic promoter design
data_sources: GEO: GSE128325 (genome-wide MPRA, K562 and HepG2)
GEO: GSE301246 (all new MPRA data from this study)
GTEx v8 eQTLs (whole blood, fine-mapped SNVs)
Human Protein Atlas v23.0 (cell line RNA expression)

data_scope.promoter_window: -300 bp to +100 bp relative to TSS
data_scope.parm_training_time: ~1 day per cell type on one NVIDIA Quadro RTX6000 GPU

core_conclusion: PARM is a cell-type-specific convolutional deep-learning model trained on focused MPRA data that accurately predicts autonomous promoter activity from DNA sequence alone across 30,607 human promoters in 10 cell types. Genome-wide in silico saturation mutagenesis reveals a complex positional grammar of transcription factor binding: activating regulatory sites peak near -50 bp while repressive sites are enriched downstream of the TSS, and NRF1/NFYA/SP1 motifs repress transcription when bound downstream. The model is 240-fold cheaper experimentally than genome-wide MPRA, detects stimulus-driven regulatory rewiring, and designs fully synthetic strong promoters.
why_it_matters: First genome-wide systematic mapping of functional (not merely sequence-present) TF binding sites across all 30,607 human promoters in 10 cell types; uncovers positional grammar invisible to naive motif scanning; democratizes cell-type-specific regulatory modeling at low cost; correctly predicts cancer-associated TERT mutations and eQTL directions; enables de novo synthetic promoter design and personalized medicine applications in patient-derived organoids.
why_nature: Foundational biological principle (positional grammar of TF regulation) at human genome scale; technical innovation democratizing sequence-to-activity modeling; cross-disciplinary impact spanning cancer genomics, synthetic biology, eQTL interpretation, and personalized medicine; open science infrastructure (parm.deridderlab.nl browser, GEO data, GitHub code).
added_value_to_my_research: PARM's ISM perturbation-predict-validate loop -> spatial counterfactual framework for causal attribution in 2SFCA accessibility modeling
Targeted-data-over-scale philosophy -> focused spatial models per catchment area outperforming generic ensembles for health geography prediction
Position-conditioned CNN kernels (TSS-anchored) -> MGWR/GWR analog with explicit distance-varying kernel for spatial non-stationarity
TF-promoter bipartite RS graph + community detection -> network-based delineation of co-regulated gene modules, structurally equivalent to functional urban region detection
Multi-cell-type shared backbone + fine-tuning -> methodology transfer for fine-tuning geospatial foundation models across city contexts

related.title: A foundation model of transcription across human cell types
related.relation: complementary large-scale foundation model for transcriptional regulation across cell types; contrasts with PARM's lightweight targeted approach

related.title: Multiomics and deep learning dissect regulatory syntax in human development
related.relation: parallel work dissecting regulatory syntax using deep learning; extends to multiomics and developmental contexts

related.title: Identification of the human DPR core promoter element using machine learning
related.relation: earlier work identifying core promoter elements by ML; directly relevant to PARM's promoter-focused approach

related.title: A software framework for end-to-end genomic sequence analysis with deep learning
related.relation: deep-learning framework for genomic sequence analysis enabling the kind of end-to-end modeling PARM implements

related.title: HESpotEx: a dual-stream deep learning framework for spot-level gene expression prediction from histological images
related.relation: complementary prediction of gene expression from orthogonal data modality (histological images); contrasts with PARM's sequence-based approach

figures.caption: PARM principle and validation — MPRA schematic, correlation between predicted and measured activities for held-out promoters (Pearson R = 0.92 in K562), and genetic algorithm workflow for synthetic promoter design with experimental MPRA validation

Single-nucleotide predictions across cell types — promoter-focused library schematic, Pearson R values for 9 cell lines plus a CRC organoid, and precision/recall comparison vs. Enformer, Borzoi, and FIMO across 6 cell types

Cell-type-specific TF regulatory sites and stimulus responses — RS counts per TF family across cell types, measured promoter activity changes under three stimuli (heat shock, nutlin-3a, PMA), and ISM examples

Positional preferences of regulatory sites — orientation specificity for CTCF and TBP, pan-promoter RS distribution peaking at -50 bp vs. flat naive motif scanning, and TF-specific positional pattern heatmaps across cell lines

Positional grammar of activating vs. repressive RSs — activating RSs peak near -50 bp while repressive RSs are enriched downstream of TSS; in silico motif insertion heatmaps for NRF1, NFYA, SP1, YY1; MPRA validation in 4 cell types
